coredata.dc:title: Inactivation of the extracellular antibiotic resistance gene by titanium dioxide wrapped with carbon quantum dots under ultraviolet A irradiation

Kang, Jun-Won
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to mitigate extracellular antibiotic resistance genes (eARG) by employing a photocatalyst-based advanced oxidation process (AOP), renowned for inactivating ARG through interactions with reactive oxygen species (ROS). Nanocomposites were synthesized by coating nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) onto titanium dioxide (TiO2) and utilized them to inactivate ARG under UVA irradiation. The transformation assay indicated that 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO₂ achieved the highest inactivation performance, reducing eARG transformation activity by over 4 log units, while 1 %, 4 %, and 6 % formulations achieved reductions of approximately 2–3 log units. This level of eARG inactivation was corroborated through qPCR analysis. Gel electrophoresis verified that eARG inactivation in the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites stemmed from DNA strand damage. Additionally, ROS measurements confirmed that eARG inactivation by the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite was due to the production of hydroxyl radicals. Photoluminescence analysis showed that N,S-CQD reduced TiO₂ PL lifetime from 40 ns to 1.04 ns, indicating enhanced electron-hole separation and ROS generation. Finally, the N,S-CQD-TiO₂ nanocomposite applied to livestock compost under 3.8 J/cm2 UVA irradiation achieved up to 4 log reductions in certain ARGs. In conclusion, this study suggests the potential applicability of the N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA AOP system in environments where antibiotic contamination is a concern.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of (A, B) N,S-CQD and (D) N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite and (C) size distribution of N,S-CQDs. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of (A) N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite, featuring high-resolution spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s, (E) Ti2p, (F) X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) patterns (G) Fourier-transformed (FTIR) spectra, (H) Raman spectrum, and thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA)/differential thermal analysis (DTA) curves of (I) TiO2 and (J) N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

Elimination of pUC19 transformation activity by (A) Control, N,S-CQD, TiO2, and x% N,S-CQD-TiO2 under UVA irradiation, and (B) N,S-CQD and 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 in dark conditions. Here, x% N,S-CQD-TiO2 represents a nanocomposite in which N,S-CQDs are coated onto TiO2 at a ratio of x%. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide. Data are expressed as the mean of three experiments, with error bars representing the standard deviation.

Degradation of the amp
R gene in the pUC19 plasmid and reduction of pUC19 transformation activity following treatments with (A) 1 % N,S-CQD-TiO2, (B) 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2, (C) 4 % N,S-CQD-TiO2, and (D) 6 % N,S-CQD-TiO2. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide. Data are presented as the mean of three experiments, with error bars indicating the standard deviation.

DNA electrophoresis gel of pUC19 plasmid treated with UVA, TiO2/UVA and 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA or restriction enzyme EcoRI. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

Generation of (A) Superoxide and (B) Hydroxyl radical by N,S-CQD-TiO2 synthesized based on the N,S-CQD ratio. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide. Data are presented as the mean of three experiments, with the error bar indicating standard deviation. Different capital letters within the same treatment group signify a significant (P < 0.05) difference. Identical lowercase letters under the same UV irradiation denote no significant (P > 0.05) difference.

DMPO spin trapping analysis results of TiO2 and N,S-CQD-TiO2 in (A) 5 mL ultra-pure DW of 2 mg/mL TiO2 or N,S-CQD-TiO2 for DMPO-∙OH detection, (B) 5 mL methanol of 2 mg/mL TiO2 or N,S-CQD-TiO2 for DMPO-O2 − detection and (C) 5 mL ultra-pure DW of 2 mg/mL TiO2 or N,S-CQD-TiO2 for e− detection. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.
UV–Vis- Diffuse reflectance spectra and Tauc plot of (A) N,S-CQD and (B) TiO2. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy spectra of N,S-CQD, TiO2, and N,S-CQD-TiO2 under (A) dark and (B) UVA irradiation conditions, and (C) transient photocurrent responses under UVA irradiation. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

(A) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TiO2 and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites with an excitation wavelength at 230 nm, and time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay measurements for (B) TiO2 and (C) N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites. The average fluorescence lifetime (τave) was calculated using the formula: (a1τ1 2 + a2τ2 2 + a3τ3 2 + a4τ4 2) / (a1τ1 + a2τ2 + a3τ3+ a4τ4), where a1, a2, a3, and a4 represent the normalized percentage (%) of the first, second, third, and fourth components respectively, and τ1, τ2, τ3, τ4 represent the PL lifetimes (ns) of these components. N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide.

Heatmap of the absolute abundance (Log copy number per mL) of ARGs under UVA, TiO2/UVA, or N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA treatments. Abundances were calculated by converting Ct values from qPCR into copy numbers using a standard curve. Color blocks represent changes in ARG abundance, ranging from white (high) to navy (low). Significant differences in the absolute abundance of genes are denoted by uppercase letters (p < 0.05), and changes across irradiation doses within the same treatment group are indicated by different lowercase letters (p < 0.05). N,S-CQD, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot; TiO2, titanium dioxide; ARG, antibiotic resistance gene.

head.ce:title: Inactivation of the extracellular antibiotic resistance gene by titanium dioxide wrapped with carbon quantum dots under ultraviolet A irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to mitigate extracellular antibiotic resistance genes (eARG) by employing a photocatalyst-based advanced oxidation process (AOP), renowned for inactivating ARG through interactions with reactive oxygen species (ROS). Nanocomposites were synthesized by coating nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) onto titanium dioxide (TiO2) and utilized them to inactivate ARG under UVA irradiation. The transformation assay indicated that 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO₂ achieved the highest inactivation performance, reducing eARG transformation activity by over 4 log units, while 1 %, 4 %, and 6 % formulations achieved reductions of approximately 2–3 log units. This level of eARG inactivation was corroborated through qPCR analysis. Gel electrophoresis verified that eARG inactivation in the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites stemmed from DNA strand damage. Additionally, ROS measurements confirmed that eARG inactivation by the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite was due to the production of hydroxyl radicals. Photoluminescence analysis showed that N,S-CQD reduced TiO₂ PL lifetime from 40 ns to 1.04 ns, indicating enhanced electron-hole separation and ROS generation. Finally, the N,S-CQD-TiO₂ nanocomposite applied to livestock compost under 3.8 J/cm2 UVA irradiation achieved up to 4 log reductions in certain ARGs. In conclusion, this study suggests the potential applicability of the N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA AOP system in environments where antibiotic contamination is a concern.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic consumption is increasing worldwide due to the misuse and overuse of antibiotics, leading to the spread of antibiotic resistance [1]. This contributes to the proliferation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (ARB), which pose serious health threats due to their ability to survive antibiotic treatment [2,3]. In 2019, antibiotic-resistant infections were responsible for 1.27 million deaths, a number projected to rise to 10 million annually by 2050 [4], and it is projected that by 2050, there will be almost 10 million deaths annually [5]. While controlling ARB is critical recent studies highlight a new challenge: DNA released from lysed bacteria may persist as extracellular antibiotic resistance genes (eARGs), posing additional risks [6].

eARGs spread resistance via horizontal gene transfer (HGT), especially through transformation, wherein non-resistant bacteria acquire resistance by integrating extracellular DNA [7,8]. eDNA is more stable than bacterial cells [9] and is commonly detected in diverse environments [10]. Its presence in water and food raises the possibility of ingestion, and upon reaching the intestine—a favorable HGT environment—it may contribute to resistance acquisition in gut microbiota [11], Therefore, mitigating environmental eARGs is essential [12].

eARGs primarily occur in freshwater, seawater, and sediments, particularly in sewage treatment facilities where industrial, agricultural, and hospital wastewaters converge and form a significant reservoir of ARGs [13,14]. ARGs that have spread into the soil can contaminate fresh produce, allowing their transfer into the human body through consumption [15]. Furthermore, eARGs can be released back into aquatic environments through fresh produce wash water or soil leachate, emphasizing the necessity of effective control measures [16,17]. Common sewage treatment methods include chlorination and chloramination [18]. However, these chlorine-based disinfection methods can increase the plasmid copy numbers in bacteria that survive the disinfection process, consequently raising the relative abundance of ARGs [19], and promoting the horizontal transfer of plasmids through natural transformation, which facilitates ARG exchange between bacterial species and leads to the emergence of new ARB [20].

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) utilize chemical techniques that typically employ radicals such as hydroxyl (OH•), superoxide (•O2 −), and sulfate (SO4•−), as well as activated oxidants like (H2O2), persulfate, and oxygen, to eliminate diverse pollutants [21]. These processes are effective in removing both organic and inorganic contaminants frequently found in aquatic environments [22,23], and notably, recent studies have demonstrated their efficacy in the elimination of eARGs [8,24,25]. During AOP, reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals, target DNA bases of ARGs, causing DNA damage that leads to ARG inactivation [26,27]. Types of AOPs include ozone-based AOP, which uses a powerful oxidant, light energy-based AOP that applies light to a catalyst or oxidant, and Fenton-based AOP that incorporates iron as a catalyst [28]. Notably, light energy-based AOPs are seen as economically viable due to their reliance on solar energy [29].

The photocatalysis AOP involves the movement of photo-excited electrons to the conduction band (CB) and the generation of holes in the valence band (VB), where the resultant electron-hole pairs migrate to the semiconductor surface, catalyzing the formation of potent radicals [30]. TiO2 is extensively utilized owing to its non-toxicity, physical and chemical stability, and substantial photocatalytic activity [31]. Nevertheless, its wide band gap (anatase, approximately 3.2 eV) [32] leads to a low energy efficiency with solar energy utilization and rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, presenting challenges for practical applications [33]. To address these issues, complexes with TiO2 often incorporate additional materials such as metal-based nanoparticles, carbon-based nanoparticles, and specific inorganic and organic dyes [34,35]. This strategy utilizes the CB of the bonding material as a trap for photoexcited electrons and the VB of TiO2 as a source of holes, effectively separating the electron-hole pairs of the composite and thus enhancing photoactivity [36].

Among these, CQDs feature an amorphous nanocrystalline core and consist of a nanoparticle composed of graphitic or turbostratic carbon (sp2 carbon) and diamond-like sp3 carbon [37]. CQDs possess attributes such as a diminutive size (<10 nm), pronounced photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, and light stability, allowing for facile dispersion on the surfaces of other nanocomposites [38]. Furthermore, they are extensively employed as a nanocomposite combination material on account of their superior electron transfer capabilities [39]. Additionally, CQDs are synthesizable from biomass resources including lemon peels, banana peels, and coconut husks, offering economic and environmental advantages [40–42]. In addition to these sources, spent coffee ground (SCG) has also been used as a biomass for CQD synthesis [43]. The previous study focused on their antimicrobial effects against foodborne pathogens under visible light. In contrast, this study applies SCG-derived N,S-CQDs with TiO2 to inactivate eARGs under UVA irradiation, a novel application not previously reported.

Due to these properties, many studies have been conducted to enhance photocatalytic activity by incorporating CQD into TiO2 [44–46]. In particular, it has been demonstrated that carbon structures can boost photocatalytic activity by enhancing the conductivity and structure of the photocatalyst, which are crucial for preventing the recombination of electron-hole pairs [47]. Various studies have also been undertaken to control pollutants using this technology. However, most previous research has concentrated on addressing synthetic dyes [48], heavy metal ions [49], and bacterial presence [50] within aquatic environments, while the control of eARGs has been largely overlooked. Consequently, our study assessed the potential of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite as an eARG control technology. Additionally, N,S-CQD was synthesized using spent coffee grounds (SCG), a byproduct of food processing, and UVA light, a component of sunlight, was employed as an irradiation source to develop an environmentally friendly technology. Specifically, the majority of SCG, which are typically discarded post-coffee processing and contribute to environmental pollution due to their harmful compounds, are not utilized, thereby necessitating productive utilization [51]. To our knowledge, the development of nanocomposites through such resource utilization and their application in controlling eARGs under UVA irradiation represent an innovative approach that has not been previously reported.

SCG served as the carbon source for the synthesis of N,S-CQD and was procured in an undried state from Dutchcoffeestory, South Korea. SCG was dried in a drying oven until there was no detectable weight change, and o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (Aladdin Scientific, China), corresponding to 0 %, 8 %, 12 %, and 16 % of the SCG and 5 g of dry SCG, was added to 50 mL of ultrapure distilled water in a 200 mL beaker and sonicated for 5 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to high-pressure and high-temperature treatment at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature (22 ± 2 °C), 50 mL of ultrapure distilled water was added to the reacted product and treated in a sonicator for 2 min. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 3 min to obtain the supernatant, which was then filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm). Following filtration, the dialysis membrane (MWCO 500–1000 Da) was rinsed in ultrapure distilled water and used to dialyze the filtered solution. The dialysis membrane was immersed in ultrapure distilled water for 3 days for dialysis, during which the ultrapure distilled water was refreshed every 24 h. The dialyzed solution was subsequently freeze-dried to yield dried N,S-CQD.

For nanocomposite synthesis, 0.4 g of commercialized TiO2 (Evonik, Germany) powder and 20 mg/mL N,S-CQD (pH 7) aqueous solution were combined in a 200 mL beaker. Distilled water and 6 mL of 99 % ethanol were then added and the mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to high pressure and high temperature treatment at 140 °C for 4 h. Afterward, it was cooled down to room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to yield a white precipitate. This precipitate was washed three times with ultrapure DW to remove any unreacted residues. The resulting N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite was dried in a drying oven at 60 °C until a consistent weight was achieved and then ground into a fine powder. The proportions of N,S-CQD in the nanocomposites were established as 1 %, 2 %, 4 %, and 6 %, and are henceforth denoted as 1 %-, 2 %-, 4 %- and 6 %-N,S-CQD, respectively.

To determine the QY of the synthesized N,S-CQDs at various concentrations of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, we employed a well-established comparative method using the known fluorescent standard quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). Further details are elaborated in the SI (Text S1). The morphological characterization of the prepared N,S-CQD and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite was evaluated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). Particle size distribution of N,S-CQDs was determined through dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano° ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using Versaprobe II (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The crystal phases and compositions of the nanocomposites were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5409 Å). Data were collected over a 2θ range of 10° to 100° with a scan speed of 3°/min and a step size of 0.02°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a Jasco-670 (Jasco, Japan) infrared spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained at room temperature with a 532 nm laser Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon, LabRam HR Evolution, HORIBA, Japan). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–Vis-DRS) analysis of N,S-CQD and TiO2 was conducted using UV–vis spectrometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 230 nm and emission wavelength of 467 nm using an F-7100 FL Spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). PL Lifetime analyses were performed using the MicroTime 200 (Time-Resolved Fluorescence Confocal Microscope) (PicoQuant, Germany). Additional details are provided in the SI (Text S2). Thermogravimetric (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) were conducted using an SDT Q600 (TA Instruments, USA) up to 820 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere. Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were conducted using a Bruker EMXplus-9.5/12/P/L electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). To detect reactive oxygen species such as superoxide (O₂−∙) and hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), 20 μL of 10 M DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Tokyo Chemical Industry, Japan) was added to 10 mg of each photocatalyst sample (TiO2 or N,S-CQD-TiO2) along with 5 mL of deionized water (for ∙OH detection) or methanol (for O₂−∙ detection). To analyze photogenerated electrons, 1 μmol/L TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was mixed with 10 mg of the corresponding photocatalyst in 5 mL of deionized water. Each mixture was exposed to UVA (370 nm) at a dose of 0.9 J/cm2. Light-only control samples containing DMPO or TEMPO in solvent without photocatalyst, as well as dark control samples without UVA irradiation, were also prepared. After irradiation, samples were filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filters to remove any suspended particles prior to ESR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring intensity of the 370 nm UVA
ce:section.ce:para: A single UVA LED chip (370 nm, LG Innotek, South Korea) was mounted on a basic printed circuit board and powered using a DC power supply (TPM series, Toyotech, Incheon, Korea) with a constant current of 150 mA. UVA irradiation intensity was quantified at six points within the treatment area using a UV fiber-optic spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048, Avantes, Netherlands). To compute the Petri factor, each of the six measured intensities was divided by the maximum intensity, and the average was then calculated. The adjusted intensity was determined by multiplying the Petri factor by the maximum intensity. The distance between the UVA source and the treatment sample was set at 4 cm. The adjusted intensity of the 370 nm UVA was recorded as 2142.82 μW/cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation and treatment methods for plasmid DNA inactivation

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli DH5α, with or without pUC19, was procured from the Food Biochemistry Laboratory of Dongguk University. For the extraction of pUC19, E. coli DH5α harboring pUC19 was cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth supplemented with 50 μg/L ampicillin and incubated at 37 °C for 12–16 h. Subsequently, pUC19 was isolated using the AccuPrep mini extraction kit (Bioneer, South Korea) following the manufacturer's recommendations. The concentration of the extracted plasmid DNA was determined using a Nanodrop 1000 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

E. coli DH5α was cultured overnight (12–16 h) in LB Broth in a shaking incubator at 37 °C and 200 rpm. Afterward, 1 mL of the overnight culture was added to 99 mL of fresh LB (1100 dilution, without antibiotics) and incubated under the same conditions until the OD value reached 0.4. The culture was then chilled on ice for 20 min, centrifuged at 4 °C and 4000 rpm for 10 min, and the pellet was resuspended in 20 mL of cold 0.1 M CaCl2 solution. This suspension was kept on ice for 30 min, centrifuged again under the same conditions, and the pellet was resuspended in 5 mL of cold 0.1 M CaCl2 with 15 % glycerol. It was then aliquoted into 1 mL fractions and stored in a deep freezer at −80 °C until required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation and treatment of pUC19 plasmid
ce:section.ce:para: 2.97 mL of sterilized DW and 0.03 mL of the N,S-CQD, TiO2, or N,S-CQD-TiO2 solution (100 mg/mL) were added to a Petri dish (35 × 10 mm) to achieve a final concentration of nanoparticles of 1 mg/mL. Subsequently, 0.03 mL of the pUC19 plasmid (100 ng/μL) was added, resulting in a final concentration of 1 μg/mL. Immediately after adding the plasmid, the sample was exposed to UVA irradiation ranging from 0 to 0.9 J/cm2 (0 to 7 min) at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) while being stirred with a 6 mm stirrer bar at 800 rpm. After irradiation, 100 μL of the treated solution was neutralized by mixing it 1:1 with 100 μL of PBS. For the transformation assay, 50 μL of either the treated or untreated pUC19 plasmid was mixed with 100 μL of previously prepared competent cells (1.5 × 106 CFU/μg) and then incubated on ice for 30 min. Following this, the sample was subjected to a heat shock in a water bath at 42 °C for 45 s and subsequently cooled on ice for another 2 min. Next, 900 μL of LB broth was added, and the mixture was incubated in a shaking incubator at 37 °C and 200 rpm for 45 min. The resulting culture was appropriately diluted with PBS and plated onto LB Agar plates, both without ampicillin (nonselective medium) and with 50 μg/L of ampicillin (selective medium). Colonies were counted after an overnight incubation at 37 °C. The transformation efficiency was determined by dividing the concentration of cells on selective media containing ampicillin by the total cell concentration on nonselective media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: qPCR analysis of treated pUC19 plasmid
ce:section.ce:para:
The CFX Connect Real-Time System (Bio-Rad, USA) was employed to perform qPCR analysis. Four different amplicons (192, 400, 603, and 851 bp) spanning the amp
R gene (861 bp) within the pUC19 plasmid (2686 bp) were monitored. A reaction mixture with a total volume of 20 μL was prepared using 10 μL of PCR master mix (SYBR Green Realtime PCR Master Mix), 0.8 μL of each primer (10 pmol/μL), 2 μL of treated plasmid solution, and 6.4 μL of DW. The PCR protocol included a 5-min pre-denaturation at 95 °C, followed by 30 s at 95 °C for denaturation, 55 °C for annealing, and 90 s at 72 °C for extension, repeated across 40 cycles. A melting curve analysis was conducted from 65 °C for 5 s to 95 °C for 5 s to confirm PCR specificity. All primer sequences used for the qPCR analysis are listed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrophoretic analysis of treated pUC19 plasmid
ce:section.ce:para: Plasmid samples treated with control (UVA only), TiO2, and N,S-CQD-TiO2 under UVA irradiation up to 0.9 J/cm2 were examined using electrophoresis. Solutions containing restriction enzymes EcoRI (NEB, USA), 10× r cut smart buffer, DEPC, and pUC19 were incubated at 37 °C for one hour, followed by enzyme inactivation at 65 °C for 20 min to linearize pUC19. 3 μL of DNA Size Marker (BYLABS, South Korea) was loaded along with 6× Gel Loading Dye (NEB, USA) to achieve a final DNA quantity of 300 ng in each treatment condition of pUC19 plasmid on a 0.8 % agarose gel. The gels were run at 135 V for 25 min, and images were captured using a WSE-5200 Printgraph 2 M (ATTO, Japan).

To assess the production of reactive oxygen species (such as Superoxide and hydroxyl radical), Coumarin (2H-Chromen-2-one) and XTT (2,3-Bis-(2-Methoxy-4-Nitro-5-Sulfophenyl)-2H-Tetrazolium-5-Carboxanilide) were utilized. For superoxide detection, 600 μL of 1 mM XTT solution was mixed with 2.4 mL of 1.25 mg/mL N,S-CQD-TiO2 (in concentrations of 1, 2, 4, 6 %) and TiO2 solution in 1× PBS; the XTT final concentration was 0.2 mM. After UVA exposure, 500 μL samples were collected at each time point, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 s, and 200 μL of the supernatant was transferred to a 96-well plate for absorbance measurement at 450 nm using a GloMax® Discover Microplate Reader (Promega, USA). For hydroxyl radical estimation, 150 μL of 10 mM coumarin was added to 2.85 mL of 1.05 mg/mL N,S-CQD-TiO2 (1, 2, 4, 6 %) and TiO2 solution in 1× PBS to achieve a final coumarin concentration of 0.5 mM. Post-UVA irradiation, 500 μL samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 s. Then, 200 μL of the resulting supernatant was analyzed using an F-7100 FL Spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) with excitation at 340 nm and emission at 460 nm.

Photoelectrochemical properties of the photocatalysts were evaluated using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N potentiostat equipped with NOVA software. A standard three-electrode system was employed, consisting of an FTO-coated glass as the working electrode (WE), an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) reference electrode (RE), and a platinum (Pt) wire (0.3 mm) as the counter electrode (CE). The photocatalyst suspension (20 mg/mL) was drop-cast onto the FTO substrate and dried prior to measurement. A 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. Transient photocurrent responses were recorded at an applied bias of +1.23 V (vs Ag/AgCl) under UVA irradiation by switching the light on and off at 20-s intervals. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted in both dark and illuminated conditions using a 5 mV AC perturbation in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz. All data were collected at a 0.1-s interval.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the degradation of ARGs in compost

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of the developed AOP system on real environmental samples, DNA extracted from livestock manure compost was treated and subsequently quantified. The livestock manure compost (GUMOK, South Korea), comprising cattle, pig, and chicken manure, was stored at −20 °C prior to DNA extraction. DNA was extracted using the SPINeasy® DNA Pro Kit for Soil (MP Bio, USA) according to the manufacturer's protocol, with 200 mg of compost sample used per extraction. DNA concentration was measured using a Nanodrop 1000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and stored at −80 °C until qPCR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: qPCR analysis of DNA treated with TiO2 and 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 under UVA irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: A sterilized 35 × 10 mm Petri dish was filled with 2.97 mL of sterile DW, and TiO2 or N,S-CQD-TiO2 samples were added to achieve a final concentration of 1 mg/mL. DNA extracted from compost was added to the solution to obtain a final concentration of 1 μg/mL. The treated solution was stirred at room temperature under UVA irradiation, and 100 μL samples were collected at 10, 20, and 30 min. For qPCR analysis, a reaction mixture with a total volume of 20 μL was prepared, containing 10 μL of PCR master mix (SYBR Green Realtime PCR Master Mix), 1 μL of each primer (10 pmol/μL), 4 μL of treated plasmid solution, and 4 μL of DW. Primers targeting 20 ARGs commonly found in agricultural systems, including tetracycline, sulfonamide, quinolone, β-lactams, macrolide, aminoglycoside, multidrug, and vancomycin resistance genes, were used (Table S2). qPCR was performed using the QuantStudio™ 3 Real-Time PCR System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) under the following conditions: an initial pre-denaturation at 95 °C for 30 s, followed by 40 cycles of 95 °C for 5 s for denaturation, 58 °C for 10 s for annealing, and 72 °C for 15 s for extension. A melting curve analysis was performed at 65 °C for 5 s and 95 °C for 5 s to confirm PCR specificity. The T-Vector pMD20 (TAKARA BIO, Japan) was used to construct a plasmid containing the target gene fragment. PCR amplification was performed using Taq DNA Polymerase (SolGent, South Korea). The PCR reaction mixture consisted of 5 μL of 10× Taq Reaction Buffer, 1 μL of 10 mM dNTP mix, 2 μL of each primer (10 pmol/μL), 2.5 μL of DNA, and 0.25 μL of Taq DNA Polymerase. The amplified DNA fragment with 3′-A overhangs was ligated into the T-Vector pMD20, which possesses complementary T overhangs, using Mighty Mix (TAKARA BIO, Japan). The ligation mixture contained 3 μL of DNA, 1 μL of T-Vector pMD20, and 5 μL of Ligation Mighty Mix. The ligation reaction was incubated at 16 °C for 30 min, followed by transformation into DH5α competent cells using the heat shock method. The plasmids from the transformants were extracted using the AccuPrep Mini Extract Kit (Bioneer, South Korea) following the manufacturer's protocol. The concentration of the extracted plasmid DNA was measured using a NanoDrop 1000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The extracted plasmid was used as a control to generate standard curves, which encompassed at least five 10-fold serial dilutions. The qPCR efficiency for the selected genes ranged from 94.99 % to 102.83 % (Table S3).

All experiments were conducted in triplicate. Data were evaluated using the Statistical Analysis System (Version 9.4, SAS Institute, Cary, NC, USA). Statistical significance was determined using the Kruskal-Wallis test, followed by Dunn's test with Bonferroni correction as a post-hoc analysis when results from the Kruskal-Wallis test were significant. The heatmap was generated in the R environment using the ‘pheatmap’ package for visualization. Differences were considered statistically significant at p-values <0.05.

Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) serves as a crucial indicator to assess the photoluminescence performance of materials and is associated with photocatalytic activity [52,53]. However, biomass-derived CQDs tend to exhibit low PLQYs [54]. In response, research has been conducted to enhance optical properties by doping CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur [55–57], which introduces new excited energy potentials that improve the transport of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby increasing quantum yield and enhancing photocatalytic activity [58,59]. Consequently, o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, an environmentally benign and less corrosive organic acid, was utilized instead of more aggressive chemicals such as high-concentration sulfuric and nitric acids, to improve the optical properties of CQDs [60]. Utilizing o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur-doped CQDs was validated via FT-IR analysis, confirming the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into the compound (Fig. S1A). Doping CQDs with o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid at 0 %, 8 %, 12 %, and 16 % concentrations resulted in increased peak intensities for both CN and N=C=S bonds in the doped groups. The aim was to identify the optimal synthesis ratio through quantum yield (QY) measurements. Subsequent QY assessments revealed that a 12 % concentration of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid achieved a QY value of 14.25 %. While higher concentrations increased the QY, these increases were not significantly different from the value at the 12 % concentration (Fig. S1B). Thus, a 12 % concentration of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was chosen for the synthesis of N,S-CQD in this study, leveraging SCG.

The morphology and structure of N,S-CQD and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites were confirmed through HR-TEM in Fig. 1 . Analysis showed that N,S-CQD possessed a quasi-spherical shape with distinct lattice patterns and a lattice spacing of 0.36 nm (Fig. 1A-B), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. To further evaluate the size uniformity of the synthesized N,S-CQDs, particle size distribution was analyzed from HR-TEM images (Fig. 1C). The majority of the N,S-CQDs ranged between 4 and 6 nm in diameter, with an average size of approximately 5 nm, indicating good size control and monodispersity. This narrow size distribution contributes to the uniform electronic structure and surface reactivity of the N,S-CQDs. For comparison, the TiO2 used in this study was commercial P25 (Degussa), which consists of mixed-phase anatase and rutile nanoparticles with a well-established primary particle size of ∼20–30 nm [61]. The TEM images in Fig. S2 also visually confirmed this size range, consistent with previous reports. TiO2 also exhibited lattice spacings of 0.35 nm and 0.23 nm (Fig. S2), attributed to the (101) and (004) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively [62]. CQD consists of a core of sp2 hybridized carbon and a shell of sp3 hybridized carbon [63], and it exhibits a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice in the graphite structure composed of sp2 covalent bonds of carbon [64]. These hexagonal lattice structures offer several advantageous properties for applications, such as structural stability, excellent electrical conductivity, and a large surface area [65]. In the case of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 1D), dense black spots and brighter areas were observed in the images, with lattice spacings of 0.36 nm and 0.23 nm respectively, representing graphitic carbon (002) and anatase (004) phases respectively. It is suggested that the lattice structure observed in Fig. 1 reflects the hexagonal lattice structure of graphitic CQD, confirmed through lattice spacing measurements. It has been determined that the dense black spots with low brightness in Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B indicate N,S-CQD, and the bright areas correspond to TiO2, consistent with the anatase lattice structure shown in Fig. S2.

The chemical bonds constituting the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite were identified by XPS spectra, and the complete spectrum (Fig. 2A) reveals the presence of C1s, O1s, Ti2p, and N1s peaks. The atomic percentages of C1s: O1s: Ti2p: N1s were 73.6: 22.6: 3.8: < 0.1, indicating a relatively low percentage of nitrogen, which suggests a comparably low sulfur content; thus, no corresponding sulfur peak was detected. This minimal peak intensity for doping materials, due to their limited use in synthesis, is a typical observation in numerous studies [66–68]. Likewise, no distinct S2p signal was detected in the XPS spectra of the composite, likely due to the very low sulfur concentration in the sample and the surface sensitivity limitations of XPS. Similar absence of sulfur signals has been reported in other studies involving low-level sulfur doping [69,70]. Specifically, in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, peak values at 284.6, 284.9, 286.3, 287, and 288.8 eV correspond to CC, CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2B). For CC bonds, they arise from sp2 orbital hybridization, while CC bonds indicate the existence of sp3 carbon [71]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) displayed weak peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.3 eV, corresponding to NH and CN bonds, respectively, indicating successful yet minor incorporation of nitrogen into the N,S-CQD structure. The detection of oxygen-containing functional groups, namely TiO, CO, and OH, is confirmed by 529.7, 531.9, and 533.1 eV peaks in the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), indicating the linkage of N,S-CQD and TiO2 nanoparticles through Ti-O-C bonds [30]. The presence of titanium (Ti2p) is confirmed by the Ti(2p3/2), Ti(2p1/2) peaks at 458.5 and 464.3 eV, respectively, in the high-resolution Ti2p spectrum (Fig. 2E).

Fig. F shows the XRD patterns of pure TiO2 and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites with varying N,S-CQD contents (1, 2, 4, 6 %). All samples exhibited characteristic peaks of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272), with the (101) plane at 25.3° as the most prominent. Weak rutile peaks (110), (101) appeared at higher N,S-CQD contents (≥ 4 %), indicating partial phase transformation. The absence of distinct diffraction peaks related to N,S-CQDs in the XRD patterns of N,S-CQD-TiO2 composites is attributed to the amorphous nature and low content of N,S-CQDs, as also reported in previous studies [72,73]. Due to their small size and low crystallinity, CQDs typically produce only a broad peak near 23.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, or no observable peaks at all. Therefore, it is common for CQDs to be XRD-inactive or appear featureless in diffraction patterns [74–76]. These results suggest that N,S-CQD wrapping does not significantly alter the TiO2 crystal structure at low concentrations (≤ 2 %), whereas excessive N,S-CQD addition may reduce crystallinity and induce phase change.

FT-IR spectra disclosed various surface functional groups of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 2G). Peaks were observed at 2920 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, and 1160 cm−1, corresponding to CH, CC, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. Additionally, bands below 1000 cm−1 were attributed to Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C stretching modes, confirming the formation of chemical bonds between TiO2 and the N,S-CQDs [46,77]. Although the N=C=S vibration band was clearly observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the pure N,S-CQDs (Fig. S1), it was not observed in the spectrum of the N,S-CQD–TiO2 nanocomposite. This may be due to the low sulfur content and possible overlap with the stronger Ti-O-C or Ti-O-Ti absorption bands in the fingerprint region. Notably, no clear peaks corresponding to nitrogen-containing groups were detected in the FTIR spectrum. This is likely due to the extremely low nitrogen content (<0.1 %) as shown by XPS, which may fall below the FTIR detection limit or be overlapped by dominant oxygenated species [78,79]. Together, the FTIR and XPS results suggest that while nitrogen doping was minimal, chemical coupling between N,S-CQDs and TiO2 via Ti-O-C bonds was successfully established. The formation of Ti-O-C bonds observed in both XPS and FTIR spectra indicates that N,S-CQDs are chemically linked to the TiO2 surface, rather than physically adsorbed, suggesting strong interfacial coupling between the two components [80]. In the Raman spectrum analysis of TiO2 and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite in Fig. 2H, additional peaks at 1429 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 are noted in the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite, corresponding to the D and G bands, which signify the sp3 and sp2 carbon bonds, respectively (Azam et al., 2021; Shilpa et al., 2023; Tadesse et al., 2020). Since CQD are structured from a shell of sp3 bonded carbon and a π-conjugated core of sp2 bonded carbon [63], the addition of D and G bands in the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite stems from the presence of N,S-CQD.

Fig. 2I and J depict the thermogravimetric analysis curves for TiO2 and N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites. These samples exhibited weight loss rates of 1.39 % and 2.57 % across the entire area, respectively. For the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite, the initial weight reduction likely results from the elimination of surface-bound water molecules and the dehydration oxidation of hydroxyl groups [46]. The TGA curve suggests that the thermal stability of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite surpasses that of TiO2, possibly because the N,S-CQD forms a protective, non-combustible carbon layer on the TiO2 surface, thereby limiting surface-to-air exposure [81]. These findings demonstrate that N,S-CQD effectively creates nanocomposites with TiO2. The successful preparation of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite, where N,S-CQD encapsulates TiO2, is corroborated by the described results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elimination of pUC19 plasmid transformation activity
ce:section.ce:para: Since it is known that eDNA contributes to ARG transmission through transformation [10], a transformation assay was utilized to evaluate eARG inactivation in this study (Fig. 3 ). Transformation is an assay in which a plasmid containing a resistance gene is introduced into non-resistant recipient cells, and a decrease in transformation activity signifies the inactivation of eARG [82]. The results showed no significant (p > 0.05) reduction in the transformation activity of eARG in the N,S-CQD, TiO2, and control (UVA only) groups under UVA irradiation conditions until a dose of 0.9 J/cm2 was reached (Fig. 3A). However, when N,S-CQD was combined with TiO2 (1, 2, 4, 6 % N,S-CQD-TiO2), a significant (p > 0.05) decrease in the transformation activity of eARG was observed. At a UVA treatment of 0.9 J/cm2, 1 % and 6 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 decreased by approximately 2 log units, 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 decreased by >4 log units, and 4 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 decreased by 3 log units, showing the control effect in decreasing order of 2 %- > 4 %- > 1 %- > 6 % N,S-CQD-TiO2. Without UV irradiation, the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite did not exhibit a significant (p > 0.05) reduction in the transformation activity of eARG up to the corresponding 0.9 J/cm2 dose (Fig. 3B). This indicates that eARG inactivation by N,S-CQD-TiO2 solely results from increased photocatalytic activity. UVA treatment alone also did not lead to a significant (p > 0.05) decrease in the transformation activity of eARG up to 0.9 J/cm2. Additionally, in another study, despite treatment up to 300 J/cm2, UVA alone was found to have no significant effect on eARG (Xin Zhang et al., 2022). This is due to the fact that DNA molecules efficiently absorb photons in the UVB and UVC regions [83], whereas absorption capacity in the UVA region is limited, resulting in negligible control effect (Sinha & Häder, 2002; Xin Zhang et al., 2022). Conversely, although no effect of N,S-CQD and TiO2 was observed in our study, numerous studies have demonstrated high degradation rates of anti-inflammatory drugs, halogenated organic contaminants, and organic dyes when treated with N,S-CQD or TiO2 under UVA irradiation [84–86]. By comparison, other studies typically employed doses of at least 3 J/cm2 or more, suggesting that the variance in results may be attributed to dosage differences, and implying that higher doses might prove effective in future studies as well. The efficacy of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite on eARG inactivation varied depending on the synthesis ratio, with the highest inactivation observed at a 2 % synthesis ratio. Compared to our findings, several studies on N,S-CQD-TiO2 photocatalysts have proposed different optimal ratios [87–89]. For instance, Jafari's team recommended a 4.5 % synthesis ratio, Hu's team suggested 3 %, and Zhang's team favored 4 %, highlighting that the optimal ratio may vary depending on material conditions, synthesis procedures, and the composition of the produced nanocomposite. Consequently, determining the optimal synthesis ratio is a critical precursor to developing an effective nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: qPCR analysis to quantify degradation of eARG during photocatalytic inactivation
ce:section.ce:para: qPCR serves as an analytical tool that can rapidly and cost-effectively identify ARGs in real environments [27]. However, quantification of gene damage may vary based on the length of the target ARG sequence in qPCR [9], and the inability to consider DNA repair within the cell limits the measurement of actual activity changes in eARG [90,91]. Consequently, this study quantified the degradation level of eARG using qPCR with different amplicon sizes and compared it with transformation activity (Fig. 4 ). Across all amplicons, when evaluating the inactivation of eARG by N,S-CQD-TiO2 through qPCR under UVA irradiation conditions (up to 0.9 J/cm2), the levels of inactivation followed the sequence of 2 %- > 4 %- > 1 %- > 6 % N,S-CQD-TiO2. Additionally, when examining the degree of inactivation across different qPCR amplicon sizes, there was a trend of increased inactivation with larger amplicons. This seems to suggest that the likelihood of detecting DNA damage rises with larger amplicon sizes when such damage occurs. Supportively, prior research has determined that larger amplicon sizes offer more opportunities for UV-induced DNA damage, with more adjacent pyrimidine dimer sites vulnerable to UV exposure, resulting in elevated gene damage [92,93]. Upon comparing qPCR results to transformation analysis findings in this study, it was shown that impacts of N,S-CQD-TiO2 on eARG control were significantly overestimated for amplicons exceeding 400 bp. In summary, this underscores that in applying the N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA AOP system developed in our research to real-world contexts validated by qPCR, primers encompassing less than half the total sequence are preferable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of structural changes in pUC19 during photocatalytic inactivation via gel electrophoresis
ce:section.ce:para: Agarose gel electrophoresis facilitates the separation of DNA fragments, allowing for the evaluation of DNA strand breaks and other signs of DNA damage [94]. The analyzed results indicated that under UVA irradiation alone, the control displayed no changes in bands up to a UVA treatment dose of 0.9 J/cm2, all situated below 2000 bp (Fig. 5 ). However, when TiO2 underwent UVA irradiation, the bands migrated more slowly, particularly between 2500 and 3000 bp, and a similar but more pronounced shift occurred with N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite treatment. Additionally, upon treatment with TiO2 and 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 under UVA radiation, a distinct 2686 bp band emerged, which intensified as irradiation energy increased, demonstrating greater brightness in 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 compared to TiO2. The plasmid DNA's supercoiled circular form transitions to a linear form upon damage. The pUC19 plasmid, sizing at 2686 bp, matches the observed linear form induced by restriction enzyme digestion [95]. Due to the slower migration of linear DNA compared to intact supercoiled plasmid DNA during electrophoresis, the linear to supercoiled plasmid DNA ratio serves as a measure for quantifying photocatalyst-induced DNA damage [96]. The absence of band alterations in the control (UV only), as depicted in Fig. 3, might be attributed to UVA's insufficiency in breaking down the highly resistant eARG. The pronounced alterations in band positions with 2 % N,S-CQD-TiO2 indicate significant structural changes in the plasmid caused by DNA damage [91]. The incremental shift in band position verifies that photoactivity within the nanocomposite escalates with increasing UVA doses. Photoactivity-driven DNA damage, leading to destruction of the DNA backbone and strand breaks, is well-documented and results in sequence deletions and chromosomal rearrangements, thereby hampering critical cellular processes like DNA replication and repair [97–99]. The results presented support the hypothesis that the inactivation of eARG in N,S-CQD-TiO2 likely stems from DNA strand breaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of reactive oxygen species in eARG inactivation
ESR spectroscopy for identification of reactive oxygen species
ce:section.ce:para: To complement the quantitative analysis of ROS, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted to qualitatively identify the predominant radical species generated during photocatalysis. Using DMPO as a spin-trapping agent, ESR spectra obtained under UVA illumination provided insights into the nature of photoinduced ROS responsible for eARG inactivation. As shown in Fig. 7A, when DMPO was used in deionized water (DW), the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite generated a distinct ESR signal with a multi-peak pattern resembling overlapping contributions from both DMPO-∙OH and DMPO-O2 −∙ adducts. To verify this possibility, ESR analysis was performed using DMPO dissolved in methanol—an experimental condition known to stabilize DMPO-O2 −∙ adducts [106]. The resulting spectrum in Fig. 7B confirmed the presence of superoxide radicals (O2 −∙), with N,S-CQD-TiO2 showing the most prominent signal among all groups. These results support the conclusion that both ∙OH and O2 −∙ radicals were simultaneously detected in the DW-based ESR measurement, consistent with previous reports where dual radical signals overlapped in the absence of DMSO or MeOH [107]. Additionally, ESR spectra obtained using TEMPO as a probe for photogenerated electrons are shown in Fig. 7C [106,108]. While TiO2 exhibited the highest electron signal intensity, N,S-CQD-TiO2 showed a comparable level, suggesting that N,S-CQDs facilitated electron transfer through improved charge separation. However, the absolute enhancement in e− yield by N,S-CQDs did not surpass that of TiO2, indicating that the key role of N,S-CQDs lies in stabilizing charge carriers rather than generating additional electrons per se. Importantly, these ESR results align well with the quantitative ROS generation trends described in Section 3.5.1 (Fig. 6). There, hydroxyl radical production was shown to be significantly higher in the N,S-CQD-TiO2 system than in TiO2 alone, particularly in the 2 % N,S-CQD wrapping condition, which also yielded the most effective eARG inactivation. On the other hand, superoxide radical levels showed less variation among N,S-CQD-TiO2 ratios, mirroring the ESR-derived observation that O2 −∙ generation occurred across all groups without distinct ratio dependency. Taken together, these findings suggest that the N,S-CQD–TiO2 nanocomposite enhances photocatalytic inactivation of eARGs primarily by increasing hydroxyl radical production through heterojunction-assisted charge separation [109].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap analysis of N,S-CQD and TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the light absorption properties and estimate the bandgap energies of the synthesized photocatalysts, UV–Vis absorption spectra and Tauc plots were analyzed (Fig. 8 ). The optical band gap (Eg) was calculated by extrapolating the linear region of the (αhv)2 versus photon energy (hv) plot using the Tauc equation [110]; αhv = a(hv-Eg)n, where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, v is the light frequency, A is a proportionality constant, and n is 1/2 for direct allowed transitions, applicable to both N,S-CQDs and TiO2 in this case. The estimated Eg values were 3.27 eV for N,S-CQD and 3.25 eV for TiO2. The band gap (3.27 eV) of N,S-CQDs synthesized in this study is consistent with previous reports showing that biomass-derived CQDs often exhibit relatively large band gaps (≥ 3.2 eV) [111,112]. Such high values have frequently been associated with strong quantum confinement effects occurring at particle sizes below approximately 6 nm [113]. as well as the presence of oxygenated surface functional groups inherited from lignocellulosic precursors. In this context, the observed band gap may plausibly be attributed to the small particle size and surface chemistry of the synthesized N,S-CQDs [114,115]. Given these values, both N,S-CQDs and TiO2 are theoretically expected to absorb UVA light (∼380 nm), supporting their potential for photocatalytic activation under UVA irradiation. These results support the photocatalytic behavior observed in earlier experiments (Fig. 3), particularly the effective eARG inactivation achieved only by the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite under UVA irradiation. Although both N,S-CQDs and TiO2 possess suitable bandgaps for UVA excitation, the lack of measurable activity when applied individually may be attributed to the limited UVA dose (0.9 J/cm2), which may not have been sufficient to induce observable photocatalytic effects within the short treatment time. Additionally, even when excited, the standalone materials likely suffered from inefficient charge separation and rapid electron-hole recombination. In contrast, the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite formed by surface wrapping of N,S-CQDs onto TiO2 enhances interfacial charge transfer and suppresses recombination, thereby enabling significant photocatalytic activity even under limited UVA exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of charge separation efficiency through photoluminescence and electrochemical analyses
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the underlying cause of the increased ROS generation observed in N,S-CQD-TiO2, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response measurements were conducted under both dark and UVA irradiation to evaluate the charge separation and transport behavior of the photocatalysts. These methods are widely used in photocatalytic studies due to their sensitivity to interfacial charge dynamics [116,117]. As shown in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 9A and B), the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited the smallest semicircle radius among all groups, indicating the lowest charge transfer resistance (Rct). Even under dark conditions (Fig. 9A), Rct was smaller than that of TiO2, suggesting that N,S-CQDs improved the conductivity of the composite and facilitated electron flow [30]. This can be attributed to the presence of surface functional groups and defect sites introduced during doping [118,119]. Upon UVA irradiation (Fig. 9B), all samples showed a further reduction in Rct, indicating enhanced charge separation and accelerated electron transfer under light exposure [120]. Notably, the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite uniquely exhibited a hook-like feature at the low-frequency region of the Nyquist plot, characterized by negative values in the imaginary impedance component. This negative capacitance behavior indicates efficient separation and rapid transfer of photogenerated charges under UVA irradiation [121], further confirming the superior photocatalytic performance of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite. Consistent with the EIS results, transient photocurrent response measurements (Fig. 9C) revealed that the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite generated the highest photocurrent density (∼180 μA/cm2) upon UVA irradiation, compared to TiO2 (∼40 μA/cm2) and N,S-CQD alone (∼60 μA/cm2). The sharp and stable current responses under repeated light on/off cycles confirm the effective light harvesting and rapid charge separation capability of the nanocomposite [122,123]. These findings suggest that N,S-CQDs, when wrapped onto the surface of TiO2 to form a nanocomposite, act as efficient electron mediators. This configuration enhances charge transfer kinetics, facilitates separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and suppresses recombination, ultimately leading to improved photocatalytic efficiency. These findings offer insight into the improved photocatalytic activity of the N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite, suggesting that N,S-CQDs serve to trap electrons, thereby preventing electron-hole recombination and enhancing the ROS generation efficiency. These findings suggest that N,S-CQDs, when wrapped onto the surface of TiO2, act as efficient electron mediators. This configuration enhances charge transfer kinetics, facilitates separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and suppresses recombination, ultimately leading to improved photocatalytic efficiency [80,109]. This is further supported by the presence of Ti-O-C bonding observed in XPS and FTIR spectra, indicating chemical linkage between CQDs and TiO2 [124,125]. Additionally, the reduced Rct in EIS and enhanced photocurrent confirm improved interfacial charge transfer—key characteristics of heterojunction systems [126,127]. Moreover, numerous studies have reported that carbon-based quantum dots such as CQDs and GQDs, owing to their nanoscale size, π-conjugated structure, and abundant functional groups, tend to form heterojunctions with TiO2 when synthesized via hydrothermal methods. These heterojunctions facilitate effective charge separation and are consistent with the observations made in this study [128–130].

Upon illumination, photocatalysts convert incoming photons into excitons or electron/hole pairs, which can either recombine and dissipate energy as heat or migrate to the surface of the material, initiating redox reactions and generating ROS [131]. The efficiency of ROS generation is understood to correlate directly with the electron-hole separation efficiency in the photocatalyst and can be quantified by examining the migration and transfer efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers [132]. Consequently, we assessed the photoluminescence (PL) lifetimes and spectra to investigate the causes of enhanced ROS generation in N,S-CQD-TiO2 (Fig. 10 ). As shown in Fig. 10A, the average PL lifetime of pristine TiO2 was approximately 40 ns, indicating a high probability of electron–hole recombination. In contrast, the PL lifetime for N,S-CQD-TiO2 significantly decreased to 1.04 ns. This sharp reduction suggests rapid electron transfer from TiO2 to N,S-CQD, thereby minimizing charge recombination. Furthermore, a reduction in PL intensity was observed when N,S-CQD was incorporated with TiO2, as shown in the PL spectra (Fig. 10). This attenuation can be attributed to the electrons in the VB of TiO2 being excited to the CB, where they transferred to the N,S-CQD while the holes remained in the VB of TiO2. Here, N,S-CQD acted as a reservoir, capturing the emitted electrons from TiO2 and inhibiting electron-hole recombination in TiO2 [133].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA AOP system for eARG inactivation in real compost
ce:section.ce:para: Compost functions as a reservoir for resistant microbial taxa and resistance genes, and its application to land facilitates the transfer of eARG into soil and aquatic environments, potentially exerting significant impacts on ecosystem [134,135]. This underscores the critical need to effectively monitor and control ARG within compost. Based on the results of previous plasmid DNA qPCR experiments, where primers targeting less than half of the total sequence improved detection accuracy, primers covering less than half of each gene's total sequence were applied to ensure effective detection in real compost samples and to evaluate the inactivation of eARG (Fig. 11 ). Analysis revealed the presence of seven ARGs among the 20 targeted genes, which included four tetracycline resistance genes (tetA, tetB, tetD, tetX), two sulfonamide resistance genes (sul1, sul2), two quinolone resistance genes (qnrD, qnrS), three beta-lactam resistance genes (blaTEM, blaCTX, blaSHV), two macrolide resistance genes (ermB, ermC), two aminoglycoside resistance genes (strB, aadA1), three multidrug resistance genes (oqxA, mexF, floR), and two vancomycin resistance genes (vanA, vanB). The detected ARGs (tetA, tetX, sul2, strB, aadA1, mexF, floR) are commonly found in agricultural systems, with varying abundances across the identified gene [136–138]. Notably, tetracycline resistance (tetX) and aminoglycoside resistance genes (strB, aadA1) were dominant in the compost samples, consistent with previous studies reporting their higher abundances in compost due to the widespread use of these antibiotics in agricultural and veterinary practices [139,140]. When the inactivation of these seven ARGs was assessed under a UVA dose of 3.8 J/cm2, UVA treatment alone did not result in a significant (p > 0.05) log reduction in any ARG. However, TiO2 under UVA irradiation demonstrated significant (p < 0.05) log reductions in tetX, strB, aadA1, mexF, and floR, while no significant reductions were observed in tetA and sul2. In contrast, N,S-CQD-TiO2 treatment under UVA irradiation exhibited significantly (p < 0.05) higher log reductions compared to TiO2 across all ARGs. Specifically, N,S-CQD-TiO2 under UVA irradiation resulted in approximately 3 log reductions in tetA, tetX, and strB, around 2 log reductions in mexF and floR, and about 4 log reductions in sul2 and aadA1. In conclusion, this experiment demonstrated the effective control of ARGs present in real environmental samples, providing strong evidence to support the potential applicability of the N,S-CQD-TiO2/UVA AOP system in environments where antibiotic contamination is a concern.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite was fabricated by encapsulating TiO2 with N,S-CQD synthesized from the by-product of processed coffee grounds.
When N,S-CQD was incorporated onto TiO2, forming nanocomposites, an enhanced eARG inactivation effect was observed compared to bare TiO2.

Analysis of the mechanism revealed that N,S-CQDs effectively captured electrons emitted from TiO2, reduced electron-hole pair recombination, and consequently escalated ROS production, culminating in eARG inactivation.
This high photocatalytic activity is presumably attributed to the formation of a heterojunction between N,S-CQDs and TiO2, as indirectly supported by spectroscopic and electrochemical analyses.
This study introduces the innovative application of N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposites containing N,S-CQD derived from food by-products as potential eARG control technology under UVA irradiation.

It has been demonstrated that the technology combining N,S-CQD-TiO2 nanocomposite with UVA efficiently controls eARG in an environmentally friendly manner through resource utilization. Furthermore, this approach was confirmed to effectively control eARG present in actual compost, highlighting its potential for real world applications.
